speaker: interviewer
text: We've got [INAUDIBLE] for like 30 seconds, then we can-- then we can start. Can you close the kitchen door? Sure. And phone's off the hook.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Phone's off too, but close it. The refrigerator gets noisy.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That door is closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Closed that door. OK. Wait. This is a noisemaker. There. Survivor of the Shoah interview, October 26, 1994. Survivor Emilie Stern, interviewer Dana Schwartz, in the city of Pacific Palisades in Los Angeles County. The interview will be conducted in English. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Milie, begin by introducing yourself, and tell us a bit about your childhood.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Milie Stern, or Emilie Stern. My maiden name was Stofkooper. And I was born in Holland, in a small town called Enschede. It was an industrial city near the German border. It was a truly, truly small town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your grandparents there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My grandparents lived in the same town. I had a lot of relatives there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you know about your grandparents and history, even before them?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I know is that my grandfathers were Dutch born, and both my grandmothers were German born. The Stofkooper family is written up in the history of Enschede, actually. There was a fire at one time, and allegedly somebody ran into the temple and saved the Torahs. So this is something we're always very proud of pointing out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't have a year on that, no. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the 20th century, or in the 19th?

speaker: interviewee
text: Must have been in the 20th century. Early 20th century, I would imagine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what you remember about your grandparents.

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of love. A great deal of love. My father's parents, we visited at least once a week. And this was done-- we walked or we went on a bicycle that-- And the family would get together. And my cousins would be there. We'd sit on a couch together and the adults would visit. My other grandfather, I have very few memories of, because I didn't see him past my fifth birthday. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He and my grandmother divorced in the mid '30s. All I remember of him and-- is that this was the man who built a dollhouse for me, which I still want. It's-- but he did not survive the Holocaust. So I have very vague memories. I had some great-grandparents that I remember. My father's grandfather I knew who was in his high 70s. And I was petrified of him. He had a wooden leg, and he had a long white beard. And as a little girl, I remember, this was very frightening. And on my mother's side, I remember my great-grandmother, who lived in Germany, and I remember visiting her when I was about four, four and a half. And I have a picture, four-generation snapshot, that I may be able to show you. And there was a lot of interaction, family-wise.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they very religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all. Not at all. We observed high holidays. I went to Hebrew school, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] would go to services because I would see my friends there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But no. We would light candles at Hanukkah. But other than that, we were very much assimilated.

speaker: interviewer
text: You spoke Dutch, not German.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- but I heard German because of some of my grandparents, and understood it, of course. But no, we spoke Dutch at home. Only Dutch. And we were Jewish but Dutch, and it was about equal, if that makes any sense. We weren't isolated as Jews, at least not before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were most of your friends Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your parents' friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a mixture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had playmates and schoolmates that were non-Jewish and some Jewish. There was-- it didn't seem to matter. And of course, I had a lot of cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- well, my parents were very young when they got married and were very young when they had me. Very hard workers. Very loving parents. Very caring. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They had their own bicycle shop. And-- but there was always time. First, for me, I was an only child till I was five. And then I got a baby brother. Pardon me. Weekends, Sundays were family days. We spent the whole day. We would go biking 100 kilometers, whatever. Picnic. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was-- it was a very happy childhood. Very good childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what year were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in 1932.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when things started to become frightening? When things changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I remember it. That it was something that happened gradually in my life. I remember going with my parents to Germany to visit relatives and being told to not react to the other passengers on the train that were speaking German-- which of course I would understand-- and to only speak to my parents, only speak Dutch. And having a sense of danger. After that, there-- there were some of the relatives that my father helped smuggle across the border into Holland. And so there was, I guess from the late '30s on, a sense of imminent danger.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he smuggle them?

speaker: interviewee
text: He helped them cross-- we lived in a border city. So he knew all the ins and outs, and he knew where to-- the weak links were in the border. And he would tell them to be there at the German side, and he would get them across and help them into Holland. And they would resettle and relocate. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they survive better that way, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. Several of them went to the United States before the war broke out in Holland. So they certainly survived. Other relatives who weren't as fortunate ended up in the Warsaw ghetto because they were-- one aunt was married to a Polish-born husband. And I remember my parents sending care packages. It was possible, in those days still, to do it from Holland. So there was that background of there is war, and it isn't far from here.

speaker: interviewer
text: When do you remember the war actually breaking out?

speaker: interviewee
text: The war broke out in May, 1940. May 10, 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, very clearly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember my mother waking me up. I had a friend sleep over. And I remember my mother waking us up and being in tears and said, the Germans have entered Holland, and we're at war. And my father had been-- well, had been in the army and was in the reserve. So we were afraid he might be called to duty. He was-- as it turned out, he wasn't. The war was-- the fighting was over within days, because the Germans just bombed, and Holland capitulated. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yes, it was-- it's a very, very strong date in my memory. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From then on, it was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you knew that-- that the happiness was somewhat had evaporated. That there was danger lurking. You saw Dutch in uniforms that you weren't familiar with. There were hidden buttons behind lapels that all of a sudden were shown, because they were Nazi collaborators. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it impact on you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the immediate impact came when I was taken out of school. And I loved going to school. I adored it. I had teachers that I adored. I just-- it was something I looked forward to every day. And one Saturday morning, one of my classmates, who was also Jewish, came because she wasn't-- came into class, because she wasn't allowed to go to class on the Sabbath. And she said, I am here to pick up my books, because I may no longer attend a school. And of course, I heard this. And when I came home, indeed, there was a notice that as of that day, I was no longer a student of my neighborhood school. And that was devastating.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you supposed to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to attend-- all the Jewish children were to attend a particular school that was somewhere in the middle of the city. And they recruited some former teachers or retired teachers, whatever. And it was a very long distance for me to go. We were also told we were no longer allowed to ride our bikes. So I had to either walk, or I could use a scooter. And from one day to the next, I became number one hypochondriac. I had, every morning, a stomachache. I couldn't go to school. Days that I went to school, I got physically ill at school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you frightened of?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I was-- I was not so much frightened as I felt truly displaced and-- and pulled out of my environment, away from my friends. I was among total strangers. I was forced to do something. And that lasted almost a year. Close to a year. In the meantime, we had more problems at home. We had mobs that had thrown rocks into windows. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They congregated at night and were trying to demolish my parents' business. I remember I had-- I slept upstairs, in the front of the-- of the house. And I remember a rock coming into the bedroom and more or less shearing right by my head. So these are vivid memories still, which I haven't actually thought about much until now. I've suppressed them. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Pretty soon after that, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were-- we had limitations, as far as the hours that we were allowed out. So I saw less of my relatives, and-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and some never again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you thinking of anyone special right now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm thinking of some of my cousins, my aunts and uncles, my grandfather, that I never-- never saw again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what became of your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's parents went into hiding, and they survived the war. And my maternal grandmother survived the war. My maternal grandfather perished in a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you said that they went into hiding, can you tell me a little bit about the kind of hiding they did? Where did they hide? Who hid them?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparents, I really don't have too many details. I just know they were in hiding. My father made sure there were the rationing cards for them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Whether they were in a attic, whether they-- I think they were in a room someplace. There was never talked about. It wasn't-- we weren't allowed to talk about anything. We have never discussed the war years. And to this day, I somehow feel my parents' hand in front of my mouth. And this has passed. This is over. We go on. So doing-- doing this interview is-- it's difficult. It's the first. It's the first time I've ever spoken.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Milie, it wasn't just that your mother kept you quiet during the war. It was also after the war that she kept you quiet?

speaker: interviewee
text: To this day, we do not speak about it. I tried to get my mother to participate in this interview. She cannot, will not. She's elderly now. She's frail. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I find with most of my relatives and my close friends that are survivors, I don't know their story. They do not know my story. We don't-- we haven't talked about it. We somehow had to go on. Pick up and you go on. And we have things to do. That's over with.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me what happened to you when you had to be in that funny school and when things changed dramatically. Go on with that part.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, shortly-- around that time, the Germans started rounding up the men. And during one particular night, we had a knock on the door, and it was a Jew-- young Jewish woman who lived around the corner from us. And she-- she yelled at my father, go! They've just picked up my-- my brother, and they're on their way to your house. Run. So he did. And he was very fortunate. They didn't get him. From that day on, for a whole year, my father never slept at home. He stayed with what became our rescuer, who was a former army buddy of my father, and who had stopped by one day and said, listen-- my dad's name was Leo-- he says, Leo, you better remember, you know, we are here. We're there, and we're there for you. We're there for your family. So my father slept there every night. And by the middle of '42, my brother and I were-- went in hiding. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the same place?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not yet. Not at that time. We were told-- one day, my parents said to me, we want you to go, take your scooter, take your brother, and go to our accountant's house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Don't look back. Just pretend you're going for pleasure somewhere. But remember, you're not going to come home tonight. Somebody's going to take care of you. You will-- they will try to save you. And you are 10 years old now, so you're responsible for your five-year-old brother. So we went. And from there, we were, the next day, taken to another hiding place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were in a town-- small town near Utrecht. A town called Zeist. And I-- we were there a few weeks. And I-- I have truly blocked names. It was exceedingly difficult. Very hard. And for some reason, we had to move from there. And I-- I think it was a matter of this was a couple without children, and having two children around all of a sudden was a major distraction. And we came back to Enschede, to our hometown. We were taken by bicycle. And I remember being petrified to see somebody who might know us. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We were taken to another address. And we're told, once they see the other, we'll go to another-- to another house. And these were farmers. One-- this was the family Oosterfeld and they were wonderful people. And I pleaded with them, and I begged them. And I said, you cannot separate us. Please do not separate us. I have promised my parents. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] They consented to keep both of us. And we were told that there is a bedroom. There is a little girl in the bed. Just crawl in bed with her. And we did. And we spent six months there. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I remember, my brother, every night-- he was so little-- and every night he would say-- we would go to bed together, and he would say, Milie, [DUTCH], which means 'hold me.' And I would. And we would say our Hebrew prayer that I had learned in-- in Hebrew school. And for some reason, I guess I felt that was, at that time, my link to being Jewish. And we would whisper it at night. And we had a certain freedom there. We were allowed on the farm. I was allowed to help. I learned how to plant potatoes, how to scrub floors, how to do all sorts of things that I didn't know I could do. It wasn't until fairly recently that I discovered that the family Oosterfeld never took a penny as-- for our stay with them. They were unbelievable parent-- people. I-- as a slip of the tongue, I call them parents. And I'm still in contact with Mrs. Oosterfeld, and she's been here. And she has been recognized as a righteous Gentile. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Six months after we were there, we were seen by somebody who got suspicious and who went to the gestapo. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And there came a notice for Mr. Oosterfeld to please come to the gestapo headquarters. They had some questions for him. And we knew that somebody had seen my brother, who was very dark haired, dark complexion, and looked anything but a little Aryan boy. And we went under the guise of we were children from Rotterdam, and Rotterdam had been bombed. While Mr. Oosterfeld was questioned, we were put-- his wife took us on the back of his bike-- her bicycle, and someone else helped her and got us out of there. Took us to another hiding place, to a safe house. Mr. Oosterfeld was questioned about the children in his house. And he said, these are refugees from Rotterdam. And the way we had memorized the information about Rotterdam-- because I'd never been there-- was-- but I had played Monopoly. And the Dutch Monopoly had streets and certain areas from Rotterdam in it. So I always felt somebody asked me where did you live, I would have an answer. It never came to that. But somehow, I felt a sense of security in that. Mr. Oosterfeld was told that he should bring the children that were in his house to the gestapo. They wanted to talk to them. And they let him go. They let him go home. And we had left by then. And he went-- after he had gone home, he went back to them and said, listen, I'm terribly sorry. I would like to comply with your request. But they have-- some social workers have been there, and these children are going to be reunited with their family, or they're going back to Rotterdam. They can go home. They were not at the house anymore when I came back. And at the risk of his own life, obviously. And miracle of miracles, they let him go. They said-- they did not hold him. And two days later I think it was, we were actually reunited [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where had they been in that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had been in several hiding places. They had been in a small room in our hometown. And in retrospect, when I think about the courage they must have had to let their children go, it is incomprehensible. And the agony that not knowing what-- what-- is happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: You also didn't--
